rationale_for_baseline_tier: A high-frequency watery-diarrhoea flare of known microscopic colitis with only mild dehydration (normal urine, clear mentation, mild postural dizziness) can be managed with oral rehydration and safety-netting — a T2 baseline. Because she is an older adult living alone, the threshold to escalate to same-day face-to-face review (T3) or ED (T4) for progressive dehydration is deliberately low.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No urine output, near-syncope on standing, or confusion
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend ED promptly for IV fluids
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates significant dehydration not correctable orally.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you stop passing urine, feel like you'll faint when you stand, or become confused, go to the emergency department for fluids.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Bright red or black tarry stools, or high fever/rigors
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests GI bleeding or infective/ischaemic colitis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you pass blood or black tarry stools, or develop a fever with shakes, go to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for face-to-face review; arrange a support person overnight.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a flare of your microscopic colitis and you're not severely dehydrated (normal urine, thinking clearly). Sip rehydration solution slowly (100 mL every 15 minutes), take bismuth, and avoid dairy/caffeine tomorrow. Because you're on your own, ask your daughter to check in, and go to urgent care/ED tonight if you stop passing urine, feel faint, become confused, or pass blood/black stools or develop a fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Reassess by telehealth if worsening; urgent care/ED tonight for red flags; GP in 3-5 days
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Attend ED overnight if dehydration progresses or bleeding/fever develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of a microscopic (collagenous) colitis flare with mild dehydration in an older adult living alone; infection/bleeding screened negative; oral rehydration + bismuth advised; dehydration safety-net given; maintenance therapy review recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating as trivial with no hydration assessment or safety-net in an older adult living alone.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Dehydration progresses overnight; the patient collapses at home with no one present.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: T2 with active management is acceptable; risk is failing to escalate if oral rehydration fails.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Reflex ED/ambulance for IV fluids before trying oral rehydration in a mildly dehydrated patient is over-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot measure postural BP or electrolytes remotely to quantify dehydration.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Use urine output/postural symptoms/mentation to risk-stratify, start oral rehydration, and keep a low threshold for face-to-face review given she lives alone.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either admit reflexively for IV fluids or reassure without any hydration assessment.
